data.title: S&P 500: Bears Emphasize Record P/E, But Overlook Record Profit Margins
data.description:
Bears love emphasizing record P/E ratios

As major indices (including DJI, SP500, and NASDAQ) are all hovering around all-time-high prices, the elevated valuation metrics of the overall equity market have become the focus of many market bears’ critique. Take the S&P 500 index as an example; its current Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio sits near 31x, as you can see from the following chart. This level is not only significantly higher than long-term historical averages (or around 15x), but also among the most expensive levels in the index’s entire history. In the past 5 decades, the P/E exceeded this level only 3~4 times, depending on how you count. Furthermore, the extreme P/E observed during the 2008 crisis and 2020 recession was caused by temporary earnings decline. Based on such elevated P/E, the bears then go on to argue the market is dangerously detached from fundamental reality.

Against this background, the goal of this article is to offer a counterargument. In the remaining sections of this article, I will argue that this focus on P/E overlooks the profit margins of the index, which are also near record levels in the index’s history. I will further explain why I see good reasons for the index’s profit margin to remain robust for the years to come. Given the higher margin—and thus higher quality of the earnings—I believe the current valuation risks in the equity market is far less precarious than it appears.

But S&P 500 profit margins are also near record levels

The next chart shows the profit margin of the S&P 500 index in the past 5 decades, dating back to the 1970s. As seen, the current profit margin, last recorded at 10.905% in October 2025, is substantially higher than the long-term historical average of 6.309% and almost double the median of 5.678% in this period. The current reading is also among the peak levels in the index’s entire history, only slightly below the temporary peak of 12.629% observed in December 2021.

So, what are the factors that have driven up the SP500’s secular expansion of profit margins? Among the many driving forces economists have hypothesized and debated, two stand out, and both are likely to persist into the future in my model, as detailed next.

Why high margins could continue

First, corporate tax rates have been drastically lowered over the years, as you can clearly see from the following chart. Based on data from the Peterson Foundation, due to the lower tax rates, the United States now collects fewer revenues from corporations, measured as a percentage of its GDP, than most similarly developed countries. In 2022, for example, the latest year for which data is available for international comparison, U.S. corporate tax revenues accounted for just 1.3 percent of GDP, while the average fraction is 3.2% among the 13 developed countries.

Given the prevailing political climate, I see little odds for corporate tax rates to increase in any material way. And as a true believer in the free market, I see a lower corporate rate as a structural advantage for the equity market. Everything I’ve learned and experienced has made me believe it is far more efficient for individual corporations to allocate resources for themselves than for a central government to allocate on their behalf.

Second, I believe the US economy has also undergone a structural shift to asset-light technology and information services over the years. As a reflection, the composition of the S&P 500 index has shifted away from capital-intensive manufacturing and retail toward asset-light, high-margin sectors like Information Technology and Communication Services. These modern firms, such as the MAG 7, which now dominate the index's market capitalization, rely heavily on intellectual property, software, and network effects rather than physical assets. This business model allows for extreme economies of scale (where the cost of serving an additional customer is near zero) and also rapid penetration to markets outside the US. You can see from the next chart below that the Information Technology sector reports margins soaring toward ~26.8%, both above its own historical average and also more than double the index’s average margin.

With the infrastructure and also technologies invested by the sector over the years, I see the scalability of these asset-light businesses to keep driving robust growth while maintaining (even further expanding) profit margins.

Other risks and final thoughts

There are a couple of places where my above analysis could go wrong. First and foremost, another key driver for the margin expansion in the past is the integration of global supply chains. This crucial tailwind has been helping the long-term relative suppression of labor costs and raw material costs for US corporations. Globalization, particularly following events like China's entry into the World Trade Organization, allowed S&P 500 companies to offshore manufacturing and routine service jobs to lower-wage regions. This trend has recently faced headwinds, and many have argued that a reversal (aka de-globalization) process is underway. Such a reversal could offset some of the positives I mentioned above.

Second, the risk of persistently high inflation can also impact my above analyses—at least in indirect ways. In the ideal case where both revenues and profit are equally impacted by high inflation, there will be no change in profit margin. However, historical cases have demonstrated the possibility for inflation to impact different aspects of corporate financials to different degrees. These effects can change the cost structure (e.g., the share of labor, inputs, and/or financing costs) for corporations and in turn the profitability metrics.

To close, let me clarify that I am not trying to argue that the current equity market is cheap. The valuation multiples of all key indices are indeed elevated either in absolute terms or relative terms (e.g., when benchmarked by risk-free treasury rates). My goal is to argue that the P/E ratio itself only paints a partial picture. It overlooks other aspects such as the quality of the earnings and also the expansion potential. I see structural forces that keep sustaining the SP500’s robust profit margin or even further expanding it in the years to come, which should make equity valuation risks far lower than what the P/E ratios suggest.

data.title: Macquarie Large Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Equity indices once again reached record highs in 3Q25, with artificial intelligence (AI)-related stocks leading the market. Since early 2024, the S&P 500® Index has set 60 record highs, including new peaks in six of the nine months so far in 2025. AI stocks have been the primary driver, accounting for roughly 75% of market returns.

The third quarter saw new entrants into the AI ecosystem, notably Intel and Oracle, whose joint ventures and long-term deals propelled their stock prices higher. The Russell 1000® Growth Index rose more than 10%, an unusually strong quarterly gain that has only occurred 20 times over the past 20 years – 11 of those since the COVID-19 pandemic. The strongest-performing sectors were information technology (IT), communication services, and consumer discretionary.

Retail investors now account for record levels of daily trading volume, exerting significant influence on the market. Heavily shorted stocks performed well, defying long-term trends, as these stocks are typically shorted due to poor fundamentals. This signals that certain pockets of the market lack a solid foundation and that investors are increasingly willing to take on risk.

Market extremes have become more frequent and dramatic. Beta, a measure of volatility, has been a major driver of stock returns throughout the year. This means the most volatile stocks have significantly outperformed the broader market. Meanwhile, stocks with high-quality characteristics, such as attractive net margins and return on assets (ROA), have delivered mixed results. Interestingly, both the highest- and lowest-quality companies have generated strong returns. This reflects high-quality stocks benefiting from AI appreciating in value as well as a cohort of low-quality companies with poor fundamentals, likely heavily shorted by hedge funds and favored by retail investors.

Source: Bloomberg, unless otherwise noted.

Within the Fund

For 3Q25, Macquarie Large Cap Growth Fund (IYGIX) delivered positive returns but underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Growth Index.

An unfavorable market environment and several missteps drove the Fund’s underperformance. While the portfolio was positioned to benefit from the AI theme, we do not manage a thematic portfolio and aim to maintain a steady risk profile. As a result, we were unable to fully participate in this rally of extremes, favoring only the high-quality tail of AI exposures. Not fully appreciating the magnitude of market reactions to what we viewed as transitory issues in high-quality businesses hurt the Fund’s performance. We think this is unique to this risk-on environment. Holding certain positions too long despite having relatively lower conviction also detracted from performance. While we cannot undo these decisions, we are implementing measures to reduce the likelihood of similar occurrences in the future.

Sectorwise, stock selection in IT, financials, and consumer discretionary detracted most from the Fund’s performance, partially offset by positive selection in communication services.

At the stock level, the largest relative detractors were not owning Tesla (TSLA) and the Fund’s positions in Intercontinental Exchange Inc. (ICE) and Intuit Inc. (INTU). The Fund has never held Tesla as it does not meet our quality standards. It frequently emerges as either a top contributor or detractor each quarter, reflecting its volatile nature and the cycle of missed earnings and deferred expectations that has persisted for years. The company tends to miss expectations regularly, yet the market habitually buys shares based on fantastical multiyear aspirations.

ICE, which operates wide moat commodity exchanges, mainly futures and options for energy, as well as a mortgage technology business, fell out of favor during the quarter. Trading volumes slowed in several areas, particularly energy, which offset strength in equities. However, we focus on the long-term structural aspects of the energy market that favor sustained commodity volatility, supporting long-term volume growth. We believe ICE maintains a defendable, industry-leading position across multiple business lines with the potential for favorable long-term trends.

Intuit, operator of TurboTax and QuickBooks, faced several headwinds in 2025, though its business fundamentals remain solid. During the quarter, the stock declined when OpenAI announced a new, more advanced large language model, which the market perceived as a threat to application software broadly. We have not seen evidence that this poses a near- or medium-term threat to Intuit’s business; in fact, it may present new opportunities. Key growth drivers, such as the shift to take market share from the assisted tax preparation category and also penetration into the mid-market accounting market, are going extremely well despite downbeat market sentiment due to unfounded AI threats.

Contributing to relative performance were stocks in which the Fund holds an underweight position or no shares at all. Meta Platforms Inc. (META), parent company of Facebook, is a new but still underweight position in the portfolio. We recognize the company has quality characteristics – like its large user base and technology advantages – plus, not owning it presents risk management challenges relative to the benchmark given its significant benchmark weight. Nevertheless, we believe Meta still has valuation and quality hurdles to clear before becoming a full position, particularly providing more clarity on its use of capital.

Netflix Inc. (NFLX) is not held in the portfolio; while it is a good business, its quality level does not justify the current valuation in our opinion. This underweight benefited relative portfolio performance. Not owning Eli Lilly & Co. (LLY), a large pharmaceutical company with a GLP-1 franchise, also helped relative performance.

During the quarter, we made several changes to the portfolio. We initiated a position in Vulcan Materials Co. (VMC), one of two major aggregates companies in the US. Vulcan holds a dominant position in many regions, allowing for high market share and pricing power, which is exercised through annual price increases. With abundant infrastructure projects in the US, public contracts are accelerating and offsetting weakness in residential construction.

Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (AMD), a US semiconductor company, was also added to the portfolio. The total addressable market for AI chips is vast and in need of a second supplier to complement NVIDIA. We believe AMD is now positioned to supply a competitive, and possibly superior, chip for inference on a price/performance basis. Additionally, its next-generation chip for AI training should be a viable option to supplement NVIDIA supply.

Equinix Inc. (EQIX), a data center operator, was eliminated from the portfolio. The company has unique assets in urban areas critical for reducing latency, especially as AI applications become more prevalent. However, as new locations open in second- and third-tier cities, Equinix has not been able to monetize these assets as we had expected. We believe there will be a better opportunity to own the stock in the future.

Outlook

There are many comparisons between the ongoing AI boom and the internet bubble, as well as notable differences. Massive amounts of capital are being spent, and hardware companies have been clear winners so far, leading to significant fixed asset investment and cash burn, all while the utility of products and applications are not yet fully understood. Among the many articles written during the internet bubble, a February 2000 Los Angeles Times story titled “A Trillion for Cisco, but Not a Dime for All Else?” discussed expectations among analysts that Cisco would be the first trillion-dollar company. Only a month after this article, technology stocks peaked and spent two years undergoing a reset.

We cannot predict the timing of a reset, nor do we expect the market to experience a period as tumultuous as the dot-com bubble. We are not questioning whether AI will be the next big thing; in many ways, it already is. However, many things remain to be figured out: business models, applications, regulation, power consumption, and hardware requirements, to name a few. It is an exciting time, and this portfolio has significant exposure to companies already benefiting, as well as others we believe will be future beneficiaries. However, we feel the market and many investors believe nothing can stop the AI juggernaut, allowing significant risk to build up. As long as the market continues to rise, we think these risks remain underappreciated.

Our quality-first approach has thrived through periods like this before, and we believe its merits are as relevant as ever. We will continue to maintain this discipline, providing our investors exposure to what we believe are the strongest growth businesses in the US - companies in dominant competitive positions that should thrive in both good and bad economic environments. As the market cycles, we believe our strategy will deliver significant benefits.

data.title: Magellan Aerospace: 'Strong Buy' On Aerospace Production Ramp Up
data.description:
Magellan Aerospace Corporation (OTCPK:MALJF, MAL:CA) is a materials and casting specialist for the aerospace and defense industry. I am bullish on the company, and that has paid off. Since my last report, the stock has gained 16%, and since I initiated coverage, the stock price has surged 88%. However, there are also some things that I do not like, which I will discuss in this report along with an analysis of the Q3 2025 earnings and an update to the price target.

Magellan Aerospace Provides Little Information To Investors

When it comes to the risk-reward profile, which I will also discuss in this report, I do like Magellan Aerospace. However, what I definitely do not like is the lack of insight provided. That already starts with even knowing when the company will report earnings. Magellan Aerospace does not provide a financial calendar on its website, and even with the Q3 results being published now, the results are not showing up on the company’s website at the time of writing, and the only way I know the company actually did report earnings is because I looked for it myself and found a press release through Businesswire. That is barely a good way to inform investors. Furthermore, the earnings release itself, as always, provides little color on the company’s performance by line of activity. The company reports by geographical segment, but that is not necessarily something that investors are interested in when trying to get an idea of what the company does. There is also no investor presentation, so Magellan Aerospace, to me, is an attractive name, but management and Investor Relations still have a lot to learn on how to properly attract and inform investors.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Magellan Aerospace?

Since Magellan Aerospace does not provide a lot of insight into the business, it may be useful to once again mention what it does:

Magellan Aerospace specializes in specialty materials, sand casting of aluminum and magnesium, allowing it to manufacture cold and hot sections for aero engines. The company also provides components and avionics for space and is active on key airplane programs, providing exhaust nozzles, winglet components, doors, and wing components.

While Magellan Aerospace does not provide an in-depth discussion of program and business line drivers within a certain quarter, we do know that with its product portfolio, it is positioned well to benefit from strong demand in the aerospace and defense industries.

The risks we see for the company are currency risks, as the company has sales and activities in Canada, Europe, and the United States. On the topic of tariffs, we note that Magellan Aerospace products are exempt from tariffs as they are aerospace components, and through USMCA compliance is being leveraged. However, the secondary impact of trade plays in the currency exchange rate sphere and could potentially be a headwind. The company’s growth path also hinges on airplane production ramp-ups. So, if the ramp-ups stall, that also gives Magellan Aerospace less growth space.

Q3 2025 Earnings Show Operating Leverage In Play

Revenues rose 14.4% to C$256.7 million, with gross profit increasing 30.3% to C$32.6 million, indicating gross margin expansion from 11.2% to 12.8%, while EBITDA increased 38.2% to C$29.8 million, indicating margin expansion from 9.6% to 11.6%.

In Canada, sales grew 14.9% to C$95.7 million. Revenue growth was driven by higher MRO activity and wide-body aircraft revenues. In the United States, sales grew 15.6% to C$73.3 million, driven by higher casting revenues, higher engine shaft revenues, and positive currency effects. On a constant currency basis, the growth would have been 14.4%. In Europe, sales grew 12.9%, driven by higher wide-body revenues, MRO, and currency exchange rates. On a constant currency basis, revenues would have increased 8.9%.

The growth was driven by higher casting revenues, as the comparable period casting revenues were negatively impacted by a work stoppage. In the United States, revenues grew by 1%, primarily driven by exchange rate fluctuations. On a constant currency basis, revenues would have been flat.

While Q2 earnings did not impress me, the Q3 results were strong, with double-digit sales growth and strong margin expansion driven by MRO demand and higher wide-body revenues. We also saw a strong improvement in margins. It does show that as the airplane production rate ticks up, the margins tick up rather quickly, driven by improving operating leverage.

Investment Case Remains Robust For Magellan Aerospace Stock

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Magellan Aerospace, the base case price target of $14.50 indicates 12% upside, with a $16.66 price target indicating 29% upside in a more optimistic scenario. For 2025, EBITDA estimates have been revised down by 3.8% but up 2.8% and 4.1% in the years after, indicating a total lift of 1.4%, while the compounded annual growth rate between 2024 and 2027 has improved from 17.2% to 18.8%. For free cash flow, there has been a total lift of 16.9% this year, little change next year, and 16.6% in the year after, for a total change of 10.9%, but we note that the timing of receipts may affect this year’s free cash flow on a heavily backloaded cash profile. The compounded annual growth rate rose from 5.5% to 11%.

Looking at the key metrics, what I like about Magellan Aerospace is the fact that margin performance is expected to improve substantially as operating leverage improves, with expectations of positive free cash flows being robust enough to drive net debt leverage to below one by the end of this year or possibly next year. So, the company is in a sound position as airplane production picks up, and my expectation is that with increasing commercial airplane build rates, we will continue to see significant step-ups in sales, margins, and cash flows.

Conclusion: Magellan Aerospace Remains Highly Attractive

Magellan Aerospace is one of the aerospace supply chain companies that has consistently shown appreciation in the stock price, and I believe that will remain that way, driven by improving airplane manufacturing volumes requiring more engine components, which Magellan Aerospace produces. The only thing I like a bit less about the company is that it provides extremely little detail on airplane program dynamics or any guidance that is useful to model expectations. Nonetheless, I maintain my "Strong Buy" rating for Magellan Aerospace stock.

data.title: Willis Lease Finance: This Name Now Trades At A 34% Discount To Adjusted Book Value (Upgrade).
data.description:
Investment thesis

When I previously covered Willis Lease Finance (WLFC), I rated the company a hold because the company was trading at a PB of 1.8x, which at the time I considered to be a fair valuation given the increasing demand for spare engines. Since my publication, the stock has declined by 23%, a shocking underperformance in an environment where most leasing stocks have trended higher. Prior to covering Willis, I covered AERCAP (AER) and Air Lease (AL) in one article, and since then both names have returned 21% and 13% in value, respectively. Now when we look at YTD performance, we see a wide dispersion between Willis and its peers. AER and AL both outperformed the market, which returned 17.56%; meanwhile, Willis lost 42% in value.

When we look at the multi-year historical performance, we can see that this name used to outperform both AER and AL, and it has only been recent that Willis began to underperform. I agree; for a short while Willis's valuation was stretched, and a correction needed to happen. Fortunately for us, it looks like the market has overly corrected, presenting a mispricing with a 34% upside. Hence, I'm upgrading this name to a strong buy.

Income Statement Analysis

Q3 2025

For the third quarter results, Willis's revenue increased 25% YoY to $183.39 million; this growth led to the beating of estimates by $27.39 million, bolstered by an 18% revenue increase from leasing, a 53% increase from maintenance reserves, and a 69% increase from gain on sales. This gain on sales was associated with a gross sale of $73.7 million in lease equipment, implying a margin on sale of 21.9%. The growth was offset by parts sales decreasing 50% to $5.39 million and maintenance service decreasing 39% to $3.64 million. Cost of revenue increased 4% to $40.48 million, resulting in gross profits growing 33% to $142.91 million and landing gross margins at 78%. Operating expenses increased by 48%, driven by a 23% increase in G&A, a 62% increase in technical expenses, and a $10.2 million asset write-down. Finance expenses increased by 34%, mainly driven by interest expense increasing to $34.18 million. The increase in OPEX and finance expenses resulted in operating income increasing 22% to $75.17 million and pretax income increasing 25% to $43.22 million, landing margins at 41% and 24%, respectively. Income tax increased by 82%, squeezing net income growth to $24.32 million, a 1% YoY increase, beating EPS estimates by $0.73.

9 Months 2025

When looking at the aggregate performance for all 3 quarters of 2025, revenue increased 29% to $536.62 million. The growth was driven by most segments of Willis's business except for maintenance service, which offset the growth by 4%. Management did state that most of its servicing facilities are operating at maximum capacity, and to combat that, they are currently developing a testing cell in Florida right near Pratt and Whitney factories, and they have recently opened a new aircraft maintenance hangar in Teesside. Asset write-downs totalled $23.77 million, resulting in OPEX increasing 57% to $193.20 million. It is concerning that despite a massive demand for spare engines, Willis is writing down the value of some of its assets. Management stated that these impaired engines are old, unserviceable, and ready to be monetized for sale. Finance expenses increased by 36%, resulting in pretax income increasing by 17%. It's worth noting that the $142.77 million pretax income was largely bolstered by $52.58 million from other income.

Operating income growing by just 1% is a tell that these massive increases in OPEX and finance expenses are starting to drag the margin expansion on the bottom line. Lease companies need to pay close attention to the interest coverage ratio, as their business model heavily relies on debt. If the debt is not managed well, it could massively impact the bottom line. The interest coverage ratio chart shows that even though interest expenses have been increasing, operating income is increasing faster. A decade ago the ratio was sitting at 1.42 and has now increased to 2.12. For reference, AER's ratio is sitting at 2.3 and AL's ratio is at 1.6. So even though I've flagged Willis's borrowing cost in my previous article, it looks like the company is not doing so bad on that end when compared to peers.

Cash Flow Statement Analysis

Q3 2025

When you look at Willis's profitability grading by the Seeking Alpha quant system, you will notice that it's graded a D-, and the reason for that is not earning margins; in fact, it's graded with an A+ on earnings margins. The problem is the free cash flow margin. AER has an FCF margin of -1%; the reason being, AER has been actively selling lease assets and using that cash to return value to shareholders. Willis's FCF margin is -39%, and the reason for that is that Willis is a small-cap that is actively trying to expand its market share in the leasing market; hence, it heavily commits all its operating cash flow to purchasing more leasing equipment. Its cash flow statement is illustrative of that move. In Q3 OCF decreased 6% to $63.87 million, which was caused by a negative working capital of $19.13 million despite D&A increasing by 21% and SBC increasing by 9%. Capital expenditure decreased 55% to $160.74 million, leading to a negative FCF decreasing 66% to $96.87 million.

9 Months 2025

For the 3 quarters of 2025, net income made a cash contribution of $101.58 million, and net working capital made a contribution of $6.45 million. Adding back another $101 million from non-cash items resulted in OCF decreasing 3% YoY to $209.08 million. Capital expenditure decreased by 32%, resulting in negative FCF of $124.72 million.

I would not advise investors to expect any inflection changes on the cash flow statement, as I believe this is a strategic move by the company to grow its market penetration. Despite negative FCF, the company continues to pay quarterly dividends; in fact, the company recently announced increasing its dividend payout by 60% to $0.40 per share.

Q3 Balance Sheet Analysis

When looking at Willis's balance sheet, we can see that the firm is using its financing channels to achieve 2 goals: firstly, to grow its asset base (reflected by a 12% increase in lease assets) and secondly, to extend the maturity of its debt (reflected by an 83% decrease in current debt). Long-term debt increased 37% to $2.17 billion, while restricted cash increased 59% to $158.1 million. Overall, total assets increased by 12%, and total liabilities increased by 10%, leading to shareholder's equity increasing 23% to $713.2 million and common equity increasing to $650.2 million.

Hidden Book value

Getting to the interesting part of this article, it is a known fact that in most cases asset leasing companies tend to normally trade at a premium/discount to market-adjusted book value, due to the assets held on the balance sheet not being regularly marked to market. Given the current demand dynamics in the aviation space, it was expected that leasing companies would trade at a discount to adjusted book value as the assets held appreciate in value. We normally measure this appreciation by the margin on sale the firm realizes from equipment gross sales. In 2024 Willis's margin on sale was 26% vs 16% in 2023, and in the last quarter the firm realized a margin on sale of 22%.

Earnings conversations below discuss how this asset appreciation has resulted in a value disparity of about $600 million compared to the stated book value.

When we account for this $600 million worth of hidden value to our stated book value, we get common equity of $1.25 billion. Given the current market cap of $820.56 million, we get a price-to-book ratio of 0.66. This means currently Willis trades at a 34% discount to book value.

On a comparative basis, we can see that Willis multiples have compressed to trade in line with AER. Looking at the growth numbers of Willis and AER and the fact that aircraft engines normally appreciate at a faster rate than airframes due to overhaul schedules, I believe Willis should be trading above AER.

Risk

Unless we are expecting a merger or an acquisition event, which we haven't heard any news of from management, it is not advisable, in my opinion, to heavily rely on price-book to value small-cap companies that are in their growth stage. The reason being, if there isn't going to be an acquisition or a liquidation event, we should value any company on its earnings power over time. Especially for Willis, management doesn't signal a tone of selling this company, and they are clearly aiming for growth; therefore, if no investor is paying attention to book value, the discount might not close. And looking at its earnings power, the growth of OPEX and interest expenses could contract margins going forward, leading to continued operating cash flow declines and persistent negative FCF. One further risk to pay attention to is the worthiness of the engine portfolio. If asset impairments begin to increase at an alarming rate, this could possibly mean that the engine portfolio is massively overvalued and it could be that price to book is at fair value.

Conclusion

Willis presents a good buying opportunity when we value its book value amid the appreciation of its engine portfolio. YTD the company has witnessed a 40% price drop, while peers and the broader market have given us double-digit returns. The title of my previous article was that all the tailwinds from the aviation dynamics have been priced in and since then the stock declined by 23%, including a 34% price-to-book discount I'm comfortable upgrading this name to a strong buy.

data.title: CDL: A Mixed Bag Of High Dividend And Low Volatility
data.description:
This article updates my review published in February 2025 in light of current holdings and recent performance.

CDL strategy

VictoryShares US Large Cap High Div Volatility Wtd ETF (CDL) was launched on 07/08/2015 and tracks the Nasdaq Victory US Large Cap High Dividend 100 Volatility Weighted Index. CDL has a portfolio of 100 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 3.40%, and a net expense ratio of 0.35%. Distributions are paid monthly.

As described in the prospectus by Victory Capital, the methodology starts from the 500 largest U.S. companies by market capitalization with positive earnings. The 100 stocks with the highest dividend yields are included in the underlying index and weighted based on their volatility, measured as standard deviation of daily returns over the last 180 trading days. Stocks with lower volatility receive a higher weight, and stocks with higher volatility receive a lower weight, with a maximum of 25% by sector. The index is reconstituted semi-annually in March and September. The fund’s turnover rate was 44% in the most recent fiscal year and 38% in the previous year. Due to the starting universe, this article will use as a benchmark the S&P 500 Index, represented by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

Portfolio

Although it is classified in the “Large Value” category, the fund has a tilt to mid-cap companies (64% of asset value, based on Fidelity’s size classification). The portfolio has a focus on utilities (25.7%) and consumer staples (20.8%). Compared to the S&P 500, CDL massively overweights utilities, consumer staples, and energy, while it ignores real estate and downplays technology, communication, consumer discretionary, and industrials.

The portfolio is well-diversified, with low company risk. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 15.4% of asset value, and the largest position weighs 1.68%.

data.title: Artisan Value Fund Q3 2025 Top Contributors And Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Artisan Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

In Q3, the portfolio’s stock selection was broadly negative across sectors, reflective of the broader performance headwinds discussed earlier as our investment style was firmly out of step with the market. However, our biggest source of underperformance was the consumer staples sector as we had a number of laggards, including Kerry Group (OTCPK:KRYAY), Philip Morris International (PM) and Diageo (DEO). Kerry is the largest food and beverage ingredients company globally. Kerry is primarily a B2B (business-to-business) company that helps consumer goods and food service companies go from an idea to a product rapidly, with taste, nutrition and formulation assistance onsite or at Kerry’s innovation centers. Though food and beverages are not fast growers, the trend toward healthier snacks is a tailwind for demand. The company’s H1 2025 results were mostly in line with expectations, but the company slightly lowered its volume outlook due to soft end markets. The stock’s disproportionate negative reaction to earnings may have been due to investors clamoring for growthier businesses or perhaps due to currency volatility as the company is based in Ireland but has significant US dollar exposure. We had trimmed our position earlier in the year on strength to fund other purchases, so in Q3, we added back to our position on weakness. Underpinning our investment case are Kerry’s strong free cash flow generation and disciplined capital allocation focused on returning excess capital via dividends and buybacks.

Philip Morris came off its highs of the year but remains one of our top contributors to returns over the year-to-date and one-year periods. PM has had success transitioning its product portfolio away from traditional cigarettes—a category in steady decline—by focusing on potentially less harmful smoke-free products that offer superior growth. Its smoke-free product platform is led by IQOS, a heat-not- burn tobacco alternative, and Zyn nicotine pouches. Since its 2022 acquisition of Zyn maker Swedish Match, a company we previously held in the portfolio, popularity of Zyn pouches has surged, helped by free advertising from social media influencers and Philip Morris’ ability to leverage its global scale and distribution. We believe Philip Morris still has significant runway for growth in the US—the world’s most profitable nicotine market and one that makes up less than 10% of PM’s revenues. Looking at Philip Morris through our margin of safety criteria, the business trades for an undemanding valuation and has extraordinary business economics and a strong credit profile.

Diageo is the largest spirits company in the world by revenue, with over 200 brands to choose from. Diageo and other spirits makers are contending with a mix of cyclical and structural demand headwinds. Growth has normalized after a COVID-induced bounce, and consumers have been trading down to cheaper value alternatives, which is a headwind for Diageo’s premium brands. Additionally, changing consumer preferences, GLP-1 weight loss drugs and aging demographics are sapping demand. Tariff uncertainty further clouds Diageo’s profits growth outlook. In the near term, margin expansion will likely be constrained, but the company continues to generate meaningful free cash flow and return it to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases. Over the past five years, Diageo generated $15 billion in free cash flow and returned $17 billion to shareholders. Despite current growth challenges, Diageo remains a market leader, with strong brands and significant scale and distribution advantages. Shares sell for just 13X EV/EBIT (enterprise value to EBIT)—the cheapest since 2010—providing us, we believe, with a favorable risk/reward.

Outside of consumer staples, our biggest detractor was Fiserv (FISV), a provider of financial technology, core processing and payment processing services to financial institutions and merchants. Organic growth of 8% came in about 200bps below expectations due to top-line and margin misses in the merchant acquiring business. Despite the disappointments in the merchant business, Clover, Fiserv’s point-of-sale system for small and medium businesses, continued to meet expectations, with volume up 8% and revenue growth accelerating 300bps to 30% year over year. Based on our sum of the parts valuation, we continue to believe Fiserv is receiving little credit for Clover. The market is overly concerned about the competitive nature of merchant acquiring and legacy processors losing market share to new entrants. We believe Fiserv's business is more resilient and will continue to grow in the medium term driven by its scale and Clover. Moreover, we believe fintech and payments are good businesses that appear undervalued by the market. Both businesses are in highly consolidated industries where scale advantages are critical, and revenues are sticky due to high switching costs. A high share of recurring revenue and profit, an attractive margin profile and high free cash flow conversion are characteristics that should provide downside protection, in our view.

On the positive side, the three new purchases we made in Q2 during the post-Liberation Day market meltdown—Lam Research (LRCX), ASML (ASML) and Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO)—were each among our top contributors to returns in Q3. Lam and ASML are semiconductor equipment companies, and Thermo Fisher is a life science tools and clinical research company. As value investors, conditions of fear and uncertainty are fertile ground for creating attractive long-term buying opportunities. Few areas of the market were under greater pressure to start the year than semiconductors & semiconductor equipment stocks. We had been researching Lam since 2023, so we knew the company well and were able to act quickly when the stock plunged. At our initial purchase, Lam was selling for ~$60, almost 50% below its July 2024 highs. Lam is one of the key global suppliers of chip equipment serving the memory (NAND and DRAM) and foundry/logic markets. The financial condition is rock solid as it has a net cash balance sheet, and the company returns 100% of earnings to shareholders via share repurchases and dividends. Shares were selling for a 17X P/E on cyclically depressed earnings at the time of initial purchase, which we believed was an attractive valuation given the compounding nature of the business.

ASML is a Dutch manufacturer of advanced semiconductor equipment, with a near-monopoly in the extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography market. Every chip on every node on the planet is made with an ASML lithography machine. The world is consuming ever more chips, and the leading edge is moving to more complex architectures, driving capital intensity higher for manufacturing. ASML sells into this increasing demand with machines that allow innovation at the leading edge and ever-increasing efficiency in trailing nodes. EUV lithography systems are the only ones capable of producing advanced chips, with the smallest nanometer values. ASML’s high-NA EUV (high numerical aperture EUV) machine can cost over $400 million per unit and already has a decade-long roadmap with customers. With significant net cash on the balance sheet and a large backlog for arguably the most important machine in the world, we have little doubt ASML will exist on the other side of this trade war. When we took our position in April, ASML’s valuation was the cheapest in years, pricing in a slowdown from a recession and other turbulence. While ASML’s 2026 outlook remains clouded by macro and geopolitical uncertainties, quarterly bookings were better than expected, and shares rose in Q3.

Thermo Fisher serves a range of end markets, including pharmaceutical, clinical, academia, industrial and applied sciences. Recent results were a little better than expected, and the company’s updated outlook on 2026-2027 provided reassurance that its 10% earnings growth target is feasible despite softer market conditions, if management’s margin expansion objectives are achieved. The company has a leading industry position in a market that has grown about 6% per year, though we believe core organic growth can be 1%–2% over that due to Thermo Fisher’s dominant positions in a variety of fast-growing end markets, like rare disease and genomics. The company has a solid balance sheet, high free cash flow conversion and a history of good capital allocation comprised of R&D, M&A and return of capital (buybacks and dividends). End markets were already cyclically depressed, but tariffs and government spending cuts may delay a recovery. These concerns allowed us an attractive entry point of 17X 2026 expected earnings. The vast majority of Thermo Fisher’s revenue is recurring, primarily from consumables, which gives us increased confidence in our investment case while the company manages tariff impacts and as we wait for end markets to mend.

Our top contributor in Q3 was Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), a long-time holding. Alphabet delivered strong quarterly results across all segments, allaying concerns about the risks of AI on its search business. Total revenue grew 14% year over year, led by search and cloud as monetization of AI solutions accelerated. Alphabet has seen strong uptake of its AI infrastructure and generative AI solutions, which are now integrated across the Google Cloud Platform. Cloud revenue grew 32% year over year, driven by acceleration in large deals (e.g., the number of $1 billion deals in 1H 2025 equaled all of FY24). As perspective, Google is now monetizing 980 monthly tokens, which has doubled since May, and the number of users of its Gemini chatbot has grown to 450 million from 350 million in March. Tokens are units of data processed by AI. YouTube also continued to grow strongly and remains the No. 1 streaming platform in the US based on watch time. Since its April lows, the stock is up more than 70% and now trades around 24X on forward-year earnings estimates. Though no longer cheap like it was in April, we believe its valuation, which is in line with that of the S&P 500® Index, is still attractive considering Alphabet’s strong fundamentals. However, given the stock’s quick run-up, which caused the position size to grow, we did take some profits to bring it back to a normal position size (i.e., 2%–3%).

A large margin of safety may help guard against permanent capital loss and improve the probability of capital appreciation. Margin of safety does not prevent market loss—all investments contain risk and may lose value. Return on Equity (ROE) is a profitability ratio that measures the amount of net income returned as a percentage of shareholders' equity. Fixed Charge Coverage Ratio indicates a firm's ability to satisfy fixed financing expenses, such as interest and leases. Net Cash is a figure that is reported on a company's financial statements. It is calculated by subtracting a company's total liabilities from its total cash. Return of Capital is a return from an investment that is not considered income. Spread is the difference in yield between two bonds of similar maturity but different credit quality. Earnings per Share (EPS) is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share of common stock. Enterprise Value to Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (EV/EBIT) is a valuation multiple defined as an enterprise value (EV) divided by earnings before interest and tax (EBIT). Beta is a measure of the volatility of a security or a portfolio in comparison to the market as a whole.

data.title: Aeva Technologies: Q3 Confirms The Story Is Still All Hype, No Progress
data.description:
I last covered Aeva Technologies, Inc. (AEVA) in August, and since then, the company has reaffirmed my view on it. In my first article, I mentioned that I believe that revenue is going to grow minimally through prototypes and cash burn will be around $10 million a month, commercial agreements with ZF, Daimler Truck defense are more of a marketing than reality, and financing further operations will require attracting new capital. I based my thesis on Q2 2025 results, when revenue was $5.5 million and operating loss was at -$34.9 million, and cash was decreasing by around $60.6 million per six months. This meant that cash reserves could have lasted around 4–5 months at that pace, so it was logical to expect another share issuance.

Since then, Aeva has announced Q3 2025 results. And these earnings are useful to update my thesis and check whether since then, the situation has changed.

Q3 2025 Results

Aeva reported revenue of $3.6 million, compared to $2.3 million a year ago. This is just single-digit growth and a sign that the business model is still not generating significant revenue. In their presentation, they admit that the biggest part of the revenue comes from non-recurring engineering (prototypes). So, we still don't see any real contracts.

GAAP losses were $33.2 million, and adjusted $27.2 million. This shows that even when eliminating stock repricing, the company still burns $30-31 million a quarter. The changes in book value of warrants and stock subscriptions obligations made net income appear positive, but remember that this gain is not monetary. This shows that they still burn around $9-10 million a month.

Even though in the Q3 report they show liquidity of $48.9 million, this is only 1.5 quarter of cash burn left, and if it does not decrease, it will become a problem and makes me question their further financing.

Balance And Financing

Aeva had around $92.8 million in assets and around $59.6 million in liabilities, resulting in $33.2 million of shareholder equity. This positive metric comes sadly not from profitable operations, but due to warrant and share subscription value fluctuations. In reality, most of the assets is cash, and it is burning fast.

As they are avoiding bankruptcy and try to finance R&D, the company has two main financing options. The first one is - up to $125 million ATM share issuance program, which allows for flexible selling of those shares. Currently, this program did not yield any cash, but given the cash burn, I believe that it is going to become a desperate move. The second one - in November, announced $100 million convertible bond agreement with Apollo Funds. These 4.375% interest bonds can be converted to shares, and interest rates are paid in cash or shares. The starting conversion price is $15.8643. I believe this pricing means that bonds are going to be converted to shares and will dilute current shareholders.

As I mentioned before, due to decreasing cash and current financing options, I believe that dilution is inevitable. If Aeva does not find a profitable business model, investors will have to finance it.

Commercial Contracts

ZF Partnership - in 2021, they announced an agreement regarding 4D LiDAR production. In Q3 2025, sadly, there are no mentions about any mass production agreements, and revenue dynamics show that ZF does not generate any meaningful flow. I believe, the partnership is still in the development phase, and we might not see it evolving for a while.

Daimler Truck - in March, Aeva announced that they won an expansion program with one of the biggest producers and that they will be using the new Atlas Ultra sensor. Sadly, the production is planned in 2027, and investment interest is only a LOI, not a contract. And due to Q3 revenue being $3.6 million, it is clear that this project is not adding any revenue.

Defense projects - Aeva is participating in a few U.S. defense prototype competitions, but they are not reflecting in revenue either. Yes, I may sound very bearish, but let's be real, there is a lot of noise, but Q3 results don't show any revenue jumps.

LG Innotek investment - Aeva attracted capital from a South Korea electronics producer, but again in the Q3 report, there is no information about revenue from this partnership. Again, the company says that LG Innotek is auditing production, but it is not converting to orders.

I do not want to sound skeptical, yet, this commercial contracts review show that Aeva is still not converting partnerships to real sales. Orders are planned after a few years, and till then, revenue stays symbolic. This leads me to the conclusion that commercial development is minimal at best.

Competition And Valuation

Leaders in the LiDAR market show how a successful scale looks like. Hesai Group (HSAI) in Q3 reached $111.7 million in revenue and made around 441398 lidars, and net income reached $36 million. It was 50% revenue growth and profitable operations. Innoviz Technologies (INVZ) in Q3 got $15.3 million in revenue, and it was 238% more than a year ago, and it also decreased costs by 30%.

Luminar in Q3 2024 started production with Volvo EX90 and announced new agreements with Japan auto manufacturer. Even though they have yet to announce Q3 2025 results, I believe it's going to beat Aeva by a lot.

These companies show that there are profitable players in the market with recuring orders. Compared to them, Aeva is a laggard and does not have any massive production contracts, and is still selling prototypes.

Valuation after Q3 remains the same - overvalued. Price/Sales (TTM) is 39x, which is absurdly high for a company, which is generating just one digit revenue and does not have any clear future towards mass production contracts. Compared to competitors like AIOT, VPG, and DAKT, which are valued in the 1-2x P/S range. This means that investors are overpaying 20–30 times.

EV/Sales (TTM) is also are 39.5, which is out of touch. Likewise, we can't really valuate it by other metrics like EV/EBITDA because Aeva is very far away from positive EBITDA, and as I mentioned the traditional valuation model simply does not work, as the company is built on hopes.

Price/Book is 19.29x, which is paradoxical, given that the company's equity is negative. In other words, the market is valuating AEVA as it would have intangible value, even though the balance structure is worsening each quarter.

Overall, Aeva is still built on hype, not on numbers. Valuation like this can exist until the market believes, but the current situation in the market (fluctuations) and after Q3 results, I expect sharp movements of around 5-10% per day.

Summary

After Q3 results showed once again, that Aeva story has not changed, it is still a promise, but there are no real commercial developments. Revenue remains minimum, and cash burn is still aggressive. Company does not have any mass-production contracts, just long technological tests with ZF, Daimler Truck.

Financial situation is worsening, cash is decreasing, and dilution now is practically lurking around the corner, as this is the only way to finance operations. Competitors are moving faster, and Aeva is still overvalued. Due to these reasons, I am rerating the stock to Strong Sell.

Investment Process

We seek to invest in companies that are undervalued, in solid financial condition and have attractive business economics. We believe that companies with these characteristics are less likely to experience eroding values over the long term.

Attractive Valuation

We value a business using what we believe are reasonable expectations for the long-term earnings power and capitalization rates of that business. This results in a range of values for the company that we believe would be reasonable. We generally will purchase a security if the stock price falls below or toward the lower end of that range.

Sound Financial Condition

We prefer companies with an acceptable level of debt and positive cash flow. At a minimum, we seek to avoid companies that have so much debt that management may be unable to make decisions that would be in the best interest of the companies’ shareholders.

Attractive Business Economics

We favor cash-producing businesses that we believe are capable of earning acceptable returns on capital over the company's business cycle.

Investing Environment

The equity market rally continued in Q3 as investors set aside their concerns over tariffs amid resilient corporate earnings, the sustained surge in artificial intelligence (AI) capital expenditure spending and anticipation of economic tailwinds from US fiscal policy and lower interest rates. Risk assets got a lift early in Q3 with the signing of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBA). The OBBA extends the 2017 tax cuts and offers new incentives for business capex and spending on research and development. In September, the Federal Reserve began easing monetary policy, with a quarter-percentage point cut, ending a pause that had been in effect since December 2024. While inflation remains above the Fed’s 2% inflation target, a softening labor market has shifted the balance of risks, and the impact of tariffs is viewed by some policymakers as a one-time temporary adjustment. It is still too early to assess the long-term effects of tariffs, but thus far, corporates have been able to mitigate their effects through sourcing shifts, productivity improvements, price increases and cost cuts.

US equities were led by large-cap growth on the AI theme and small caps driven by optimism that a cyclical upturn will be catalyzed by a Fed rate-cutting cycle. Value stocks trailed in Q3, with the Russell 1000® Value Index returning 5.33% versus 10.51% for the Russell 1000® Growth Index. The indices’ dissimilar sector weightings were the primary reason for their return divergence. The value index has a much smaller weighting in technology (10% versus 52%) and larger weightings in financials, energy and consumer staples. Over the past three years, the growth index has outperformed the value index by more than 1,400bps annualized. Over the past 10 years, the growth index has outperformed by more than 800bps annualized. As a result, value stocks have become increasingly cheap, relatively speaking. As of September 30, the value index traded for 19X FY1 estimated earnings per share, compared to the growth index’s 35X. This 16-point valuation spread compares to average and median spreads of 8 points and 6 points based on data since 1998. Due to the technology sector’s sizable allocation in the growth index, any argument for a reversion to the mean in favor of value stocks essentially comes down to how the technology sector performs going forward.

In Q3, gains in the Russell 1000® Value Index were broad based across sectors, with all but consumer staples participating in the rally. Communication services, utilities and consumer discretionary stocks were the biggest gainers. The technology sector returned 5% but was up a much stronger 13% in the core Russell 1000® Index as strength in semiconductors, hardware and electronic equipment was offset by weakness in IT services and software.

Performance Discussion

The portfolio trailed the Russell 1000® Value Index in Q3. It has been a challenging market environment for our disciplined value investment style and for active management, in general. The broad US stock market rally was led by beta, high sales growth and lower quality. As one marker of the current market environment, in a single day on September 10, shares of Oracle gained $244 billion in market capitalization—roughly the size of Poland’s entire market cap—on announced strong cloud demand related to a contract with OpenAI. While each era is unique, the current AI-driven US equity market rally has drawn comparisons to the late-1990s Internet bull market due to the narrowness of market leadership dominated by technology stocks and the historically stretched valuations of the broad US large-cap indices. Another frothy period with those characteristics was 2021, when meme stocks were all the rage. Like those past periods, we are seeing investors’ willingness to reward companies that have little or no cash flow but have the potential of large positive outcomes many years into the future, regardless of price. Conversely, shares of companies perceived as mundane or “old economy” are given little leeway, with any wiggles on the downside in earnings being severely punished. When you can potentially make quick, big returns on an AI stock, there is little patience for businesses dealing with temporary pressures, regardless of how cheap they are.

Of course, shifts in regimes are a normal feature of markets, but what is striking today is how extreme investor preferences have become. An analysis by research firm Evercore ISI found that as of the end of September, the cheapest stocks compared to the most expensive stocks in the Russell 3000® Index, an index that broadly represents the entire US equity market, experienced their worst relative performance over a six-month span since the year 2000 (Exhibit 1).

Exhibit 1: Valuation Factor—Largest Performance Spread Since 2000

Source: Evercore ISI Research. As of 30 Sep 2025. Based on the Russell 3000® Index. Past performance does not guarantee and is not a reliable indicator of future results.

In Q3, the portfolio’s stock selection was broadly negative across sectors, reflective of the broader performance headwinds discussed earlier as our investment style was firmly out of step with the market. However, our biggest source of underperformance was the consumer staples sector as we had a number of laggards, including Kerry Group (OTCPK:KRYAY), Philip Morris International (PM) and Diageo (DEO). Kerry is the largest food and beverage ingredients company globally. Kerry is primarily a B2B (business-to-business) company that helps consumer goods and food service companies go from an idea to a product rapidly, with taste, nutrition and formulation assistance onsite or at Kerry’s innovation centers. Though food and beverages are not fast growers, the trend toward healthier snacks is a tailwind for demand. The company’s H1 2025 results were mostly in line with expectations, but the company slightly lowered its volume outlook due to soft end markets. The stock’s disproportionate negative reaction to earnings may have been due to investors clamoring for growthier businesses or perhaps due to currency volatility as the company is based in Ireland but has significant US dollar exposure. We had trimmed our position earlier in the year on strength to fund other purchases, so in Q3, we added back to our position on weakness. Underpinning our investment case are Kerry’s strong free cash flow generation and disciplined capital allocation focused on returning excess capital via dividends and buybacks.

Philip Morris came off its highs of the year but remains one of our top contributors to returns over the year-to-date and one-year periods. PM has had success transitioning its product portfolio away from traditional cigarettes—a category in steady decline—by focusing on potentially less harmful smoke-free products that offer superior growth. Its smoke-free product platform is led by IQOS, a heat-not- burn tobacco alternative, and Zyn nicotine pouches. Since its 2022 acquisition of Zyn maker Swedish Match, a company we previously held in the portfolio, popularity of Zyn pouches has surged, helped by free advertising from social media influencers and Philip Morris’ ability to leverage its global scale and distribution. We believe Philip Morris still has significant runway for growth in the US—the world’s most profitable nicotine market and one that makes up less than 10% of PM’s revenues. Looking at Philip Morris through our margin of safety criteria, the business trades for an undemanding valuation and has extraordinary business economics and a strong credit profile.

Diageo is the largest spirits company in the world by revenue, with over 200 brands to choose from. Diageo and other spirits makers are contending with a mix of cyclical and structural demand headwinds. Growth has normalized after a COVID-induced bounce, and consumers have been trading down to cheaper value alternatives, which is a headwind for Diageo’s premium brands. Additionally, changing consumer preferences, GLP-1 weight loss drugs and aging demographics are sapping demand. Tariff uncertainty further clouds Diageo’s profits growth outlook. In the near term, margin expansion will likely be constrained, but the company continues to generate meaningful free cash flow and return it to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases. Over the past five years, Diageo generated $15 billion in free cash flow and returned $17 billion to shareholders. Despite current growth challenges, Diageo remains a market leader, with strong brands and significant scale and distribution advantages. Shares sell for just 13X EV/EBIT (enterprise value to EBIT)—the cheapest since 2010—providing us, we believe, with a favorable risk/reward.

Outside of consumer staples, our biggest detractor was Fiserv (FISV), a provider of financial technology, core processing and payment processing services to financial institutions and merchants. Organic growth of 8% came in about 200bps below expectations due to top-line and margin misses in the merchant acquiring business. Despite the disappointments in the merchant business, Clover, Fiserv’s point-of-sale system for small and medium businesses, continued to meet expectations, with volume up 8% and revenue growth accelerating 300bps to 30% year over year. Based on our sum of the parts valuation, we continue to believe Fiserv is receiving little credit for Clover. The market is overly concerned about the competitive nature of merchant acquiring and legacy processors losing market share to new entrants. We believe Fiserv's business is more resilient and will continue to grow in the medium term driven by its scale and Clover. Moreover, we believe fintech and payments are good businesses that appear undervalued by the market. Both businesses are in highly consolidated industries where scale advantages are critical, and revenues are sticky due to high switching costs. A high share of recurring revenue and profit, an attractive margin profile and high free cash flow conversion are characteristics that should provide downside protection, in our view.

On the positive side, the three new purchases we made in Q2 during the post-Liberation Day market meltdown—Lam Research (LRCX), ASML (ASML) and Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO)—were each among our top contributors to returns in Q3. Lam and ASML are semiconductor equipment companies, and Thermo Fisher is a life science tools and clinical research company. As value investors, conditions of fear and uncertainty are fertile ground for creating attractive long-term buying opportunities. Few areas of the market were under greater pressure to start the year than semiconductors & semiconductor equipment stocks. We had been researching Lam since 2023, so we knew the company well and were able to act quickly when the stock plunged. At our initial purchase, Lam was selling for ~$60, almost 50% below its July 2024 highs. Lam is one of the key global suppliers of chip equipment serving the memory (NAND and DRAM) and foundry/logic markets. The financial condition is rock solid as it has a net cash balance sheet, and the company returns 100% of earnings to shareholders via share repurchases and dividends. Shares were selling for a 17X P/E on cyclically depressed earnings at the time of initial purchase, which we believed was an attractive valuation given the compounding nature of the business.

ASML is a Dutch manufacturer of advanced semiconductor equipment, with a near-monopoly in the extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography market. Every chip on every node on the planet is made with an ASML lithography machine. The world is consuming ever more chips, and the leading edge is moving to more complex architectures, driving capital intensity higher for manufacturing. ASML sells into this increasing demand with machines that allow innovation at the leading edge and ever-increasing efficiency in trailing nodes. EUV lithography systems are the only ones capable of producing advanced chips, with the smallest nanometer values. ASML’s high-NA EUV (high numerical aperture EUV) machine can cost over $400 million per unit and already has a decade-long roadmap with customers. With significant net cash on the balance sheet and a large backlog for arguably the most important machine in the world, we have little doubt ASML will exist on the other side of this trade war. When we took our position in April, ASML’s valuation was the cheapest in years, pricing in a slowdown from a recession and other turbulence. While ASML’s 2026 outlook remains clouded by macro and geopolitical uncertainties, quarterly bookings were better than expected, and shares rose in Q3.

Thermo Fisher serves a range of end markets, including pharmaceutical, clinical, academia, industrial and applied sciences. Recent results were a little better than expected, and the company’s updated outlook on 2026-2027 provided reassurance that its 10% earnings growth target is feasible despite softer market conditions, if management’s margin expansion objectives are achieved. The company has a leading industry position in a market that has grown about 6% per year, though we believe core organic growth can be 1%–2% over that due to Thermo Fisher’s dominant positions in a variety of fast-growing end markets, like rare disease and genomics. The company has a solid balance sheet, high free cash flow conversion and a history of good capital allocation comprised of R&D, M&A and return of capital (buybacks and dividends). End markets were already cyclically depressed, but tariffs and government spending cuts may delay a recovery. These concerns allowed us an attractive entry point of 17X 2026 expected earnings. The vast majority of Thermo Fisher’s revenue is recurring, primarily from consumables, which gives us increased confidence in our investment case while the company manages tariff impacts and as we wait for end markets to mend.

Our top contributor in Q3 was Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), a long-time holding. Alphabet delivered strong quarterly results across all segments, allaying concerns about the risks of AI on its search business. Total revenue grew 14% year over year, led by search and cloud as monetization of AI solutions accelerated. Alphabet has seen strong uptake of its AI infrastructure and generative AI solutions, which are now integrated across the Google Cloud Platform. Cloud revenue grew 32% year over year, driven by acceleration in large deals (e.g., the number of $1 billion deals in 1H 2025 equaled all of FY24). As perspective, Google is now monetizing 980 monthly tokens, which has doubled since May, and the number of users of its Gemini chatbot has grown to 450 million from 350 million in March. Tokens are units of data processed by AI. YouTube also continued to grow strongly and remains the No. 1 streaming platform in the US based on watch time. Since its April lows, the stock is up more than 70% and now trades around 24X on forward-year earnings estimates. Though no longer cheap like it was in April, we believe its valuation, which is in line with that of the S&P 500® Index, is still attractive considering Alphabet’s strong fundamentals. However, given the stock’s quick run-up, which caused the position size to grow, we did take some profits to bring it back to a normal position size (i.e., 2%–3%).

Portfolio Activity

Though the rally’s tide lifted most boats, we’re still finding our share of unloved and out of favor businesses. In Q3, we made three new purchases: Accenture (ACN), Salesforce (CRM) and Elevance Health (ELV). Like all technological innovations, AI should create both winners and losers. A number of incumbent IT services and software businesses, including Accenture and Salesforce, are seen as potential “AI losers” that are at risk of being disrupted by AI. While AI will drive change, the fears that AI will disrupt companies with wide moats like Accenture and Salesforce seem overblown. Accenture provides consulting and business process outsourcing services to corporate clients to help them stay on the leading edge of change via technology. Salesforce is the largest provider of customer relationship management (CRM) software. Growth for these companies has slowed in 2025, which has spooked investors, but this appears due to general slowing macro conditions, among other factors, rather than due to AI, which should take years to play out. Both companies meet our margin of safety criteria as they are strong, consistent generators of free cash flow, have solid balance sheets and sell at reasonable valuations.

We swapped one managed care company for another in purchasing Elevance and selling Cigna (CI). Both companies operate health insurance businesses and trade cheaply, but their business mixes are different. Formerly known as Anthem and Wellpoint, Elevance is one of the nation’s largest health insurers and the largest for-profit entity in the Blue Cross Blue Shield Association. The company operates in commercial, Medicare Advantage and Medicaid through its health benefits segment and provides pharmacy benefits, life insurance and disability insurance. Cigna has a much larger pharmacy benefits management (PBM) business that generates over 50% of operating profits. There are rumblings in Washington about PBM reform, which should pose a greater risk to Cigna than to Elevance. Our investment case for Elevance, like other managed care companies, is its currently depressed earnings can recover as pricing adjusts higher in line with rising costs, and as this occurs, the stock price multiple can also normalize. At the time of initial purchase, Elevance shares were trading under 10X depressed 2025 expected earnings per share, compared to its 10-year average of 16X.

In addition to selling Cigna, we exited Dollar General (DG), a discount retailer, in favor of better opportunities. While Dollar General is showing progress on improving its business, we still have concerns about the sustainability of its turnaround in the face of tough competition and a weak lower income consumer.

Perspective

Amid recent performance patterns, we are receiving questions about our willingness to adjust our investment approach for this environment. The technology world is fascinated with first principles thinking, and so are we. First principles thinking is a problem-solving method that involves breaking down complex problems or processes into their most basic, fundamental truths—called first principles—that cannot be deduced further. From these foundational elements, one then builds up knowledge or new solutions without relying on assumptions or analogies. We also have our first principles. As long- term, fundamentals-based investors, we think like business owners.

We are not traders of pieces of paper. A business owner ultimately cares about one thing—how much cash the business will generate. As investors, we estimate the business’s expected annual cash flow and then compare that to the offer price to determine if our expected cash return or yield is attractive. If cash flow gets valued too low in the marketplace, capital should ultimately flow there, and at some point, markets will realize these cash flows are being mispriced.

In an environment in which animal spirits are fueled by the AI theme, we are reminded of the famous J.P. Morgan quote, “Nothing so undermines your financial judgement as the sight of your neighbor getting rich.” However, we won’t deviate from our investment process. The market’s behavior should create opportunity and embedded alpha for a disciplined long-term minded investment approach. The portfolio continues to be tilted toward high-quality large-cap names selling at undemanding valuations. The portfolio sells for ~16X FY1 earnings—more than two multiple points less than the Russell 1000® Value Index. We believe the portfolio is also higher quality as it has a higher return on equity (14.2% versus 11.8%) and fixed charge coverage ratio (8.8X versus 5.3X)—one of our preferred measures of financial condition. We can’t predict the markets or economic trends. What we can control is the execution of our process, and we believe the characteristics of the portfolio today demonstrate our continued execution of our investment discipline.
