data.title: Rising Global Demand Fuels U.S. LNG Export Growth
data.description:
By Ivan Castano

The world is clamoring for gas and the U.S. is taking heed. Over $50 billion is expected to flow into natural gas infrastructure projects, notably for liquefied natural gas (LNG) output set to bolster exports by 75% in five years, analysts said.

The massive expansion, which could further solidify the U.S.'s global LNG export lead over Australia and Qatar, will help meet surging demand for the fuel, mainly from Europe and Asia, as well as for AI data centers at home, experts say.

Project Boom

A slew of projects are currently in the pipeline across the U.S. Gulf Coast. The largest ones are Plaquemines, Corpus Christi, Golden Pass, Cameron LNG, Rio Grande, Port Arthur, CP2 and Costa Azul.

"This year, Plaquemines LNG Phase 2 is already online, Corpus Christi's Stage 3 three trains will be in operation and Golden Pass's Train 1 should be running, with 2 and 3 expected to be ready throughout 2026," Weixel detailed. This, coupled with Costa Azul (a project based in Mexico but for U.S.-product export), will bring LNG production up by 4.5 bcf/d by summer 2026, he added.

Another 7 bcf/d will come from additional expansions at Cameron, Rio Grande, Port Arthur and CP2, according to Weixel.

If they are completed on time, the facilities will elevate U.S. output (for both domestic and export supply) to 140 bcf/d by 2030 versus roughly 115 bcf/d currently, analysts said.

Export Drive

Demand for U.S. LNG is soaring, so much so that cargoes were up 22% to 69 million tons in the first eight months of 2025, according to trade intelligence provider Kpler. In October, the U.S. became the first country to export 10 million tonnes of LNG in a single month.

This has helped boost 2025’s average prices to roughly $8.00 per thousand cubic feet, the most American suppliers have received for the super-chilled liquefied fuel since 2023, according to data from the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA).

The trend looks set to continue for some time, experts said, driven by growing demand from Europe, Asia and Latin America. Europe's purchases have soared since the Russia-Ukraine war prompted it to seek alternative gas supplies. The Netherlands, France and Spain are the biggest buyers, absorbing two-thirds of U.S. shipments.

Asia, too, is ratcheting up demand, especially Japan, South Korea and increasingly, India – a market that looks promising.

"India is going to be a big market," said Weixel. "Their AC market is 14 million to 15 million units versus 100,000 units 10 years ago. They are expected to build combined-cycle gas plants to move away from coal."

Price Uncertainty

The U.S. gas crusade, coupled with another major push by Qatar, could elevate global supply by 45% in five years, according to the International Energy Agency (IEA). This could create a supply glut, depressing prices.

"The industry is entering a period of major price swings as rising global demand runs into bottlenecks on pipeline and export infrastructure," Expand Energy CEO Nick Dell'Osso told specialist publication Semafor.

While the U.S. has no shortage of untapped gas underground, it is beginning to run low in many traditional drilling hotspots best connected to existing distribution networks, Dell’Osso also said. This could create supply disruptions and price crashes amid surging demand for AI data centers and electricity overall, he noted.

Weixel was not as alarmed, however, noting that he believes the capacity add-ons will be enough to meet America's future power needs, likely keeping prices around the historical average and well below the $9 per MMBtu peak reached in 2022. As of mid-November, natural gas prices are around $4.50 per MMBtu.

AI Data Centers and Beyond

As the AI boom encourages firms like Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) or Meta (META) to install data centers around the country, their electricity demand is forecast to rise to 12% of the total grid from up to 4% currently, according to Bryson Hull of energy consultancy HBW Resources.

The build-up may not progress as rapidly as hoped, however, mainly because of a shortage of the high-voltage transformers needed to connect the facilities to the grid. Even if they proceed as planned, Hull doesn't expect gas prices will surpass the $3.15 per MMBtu of the past 10 years, as there's plenty of supply to meet demand.

As always, other unpredictable factors could drive volatility.

"You can have an extreme winter, a plant blow up, or expectations for production increases that don't materialize," said Sergio Chapa, an analyst at Poten & Partners.

"Think of what happened when Freeport LNG exploded in June 2022. We suddenly lost 2 Bcf/d and Henry Hub prices collapsed," he said. Conversely, "during the 2021 Texas freeze, all the LNG plants were taken offline and prices shot up."

Markets appear to anticipate higher prices in the near term, however. As of mid-November, Henry Hub futures are trading above $4.00 through February 2026. As investors look to manage risks tied to both seasonal and structural changes in the natural gas landscape, Henry Hub derivatives are witnessing increased demand.

"We are seeing more customers, particularly from the buy side, and banks piling into trading natural gas as it has become not just a transition fuel, but a fuel for the future," said CME Group's Global Head of Commodities Derek Sammann in a recent video.

“Think about the expansion – already [the U.S.] is the largest exporter of natural gas in the world… we are now going to be almost doubling that capacity in the next two and a half years,” said Sammann. “From a global benchmark point of view, that is very much a structural shift.”

data.title: Samsung's Price Hike Says A Lot About Where Semiconductors Are Heading
data.description:
Introduction

Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF) stock is up more than 55% today, and I’m honestly very happy to see it because I rated this company as a Buy back in June. And back then, when I broke down Samsung’s Unpacked 2025 event, I wrote very clearly that Samsung’s AI ecosystem was becoming a real threat to Apple (AAPL), Google (GOOGL), and many other players that are usually seen as the future of AI. It was obvious that something big was brewing inside Samsung, but the U.S. investors care less about it, because Samsung is an ADR, and most people decide not to buy because of it (I am not an exception).

Anyway, for everyone who follows semiconductors, AI, and tech in general, you would like to know what happened today, because it says a lot about the industry in general. Samsung raised prices for its memory chips by about 60% because the supply of DDR5 is so low and the demand is so huge that it has complete pricing power. Samsung surged by so much because of how undervalued it was, but on the same news, Micron Technology (MU) is up as well. That's because it’s a great indicator that the demand is not only high, but it gets bigger and bigger every day, because the supply is insufficient, and customers start panicking and wiping out the inventory.

Now, I know what you think. If everyone is wiping out inventory right now and panic-buying every chip they can get their hands on, then the overload should happen very soon, but I don’t actually think the cycle will repeat itself the way it used to, simply because AI completely changes the rhythm of demand. In previous cycles, once customers overbought, everything collapsed at the same time, but today, the moment inventories fill up, another wave of AI infrastructure rises and absorbs everything again. So at some point, supply will catch up and the market will cool down, but the classic boom-and-bust pattern we had for decades will likely not repeat itself in such a clean, predictable way anymore.

Price Rise By 60%

I think many investors saw the headline about Samsung increasing DDR5 prices by up to 60% and were shocked, because it is not something you see in semiconductors often. But the reason behind it is actually very straightforward. All the competitors (like SK hynix (OTCPK:HXSC.F), Micron, etc.) have been shifting heavily into high bandwidth memory, or HBM, production. HBM is the extremely advanced memory used in AI accelerators like Nvidia’s (NVDA) GPUs, and the margins are amazing there. But when you shift your production into HBM, you reduce your DDR5 output. The lower supply of DDR5 has brought huge demand for it today.

Samsung, on the other hand, didn’t convert its production as aggressively. They were slower in HBM, so they had a huge spare DDR5 capacity. Everyone on the market started panic buying DDR5, realizing that the supply is very limited, but they need to feed their data centers, and Samsung is practically the only huge producer that still has meaningful capacity left. As a result, the company ends up with unbelievable pricing power.

The Undervaluation

So the question is whether Samsung will stay at these levels. Even after a fifty percent jump in a single day, I still think Samsung is extremely cheap when you look at what this company actually owns and controls. Samsung is a giant in memory, smartphones, displays, foundry, sensors, home electronics, and now one of the biggest emerging forces in AI infrastructure. The stock was majorly undervalued for a very long time, and this repricing is simply the market realizing what Samsung has to bring to the table.

Besides, Samsung is not relying only on the current DDR5 shortage. Just two weeks ago, Samsung and Nvidia announced a huge collaboration to build an AI factory powered by more than 50,000 Nvidia GPUs. This factory is designed specifically to support Samsung’s upcoming HBM4 production. And Samsung already said it is in active discussions to supply HBM4 to Nvidia. SK hynix currently dominates Nvidia’s HBM supply. Micron is catching up, but Micron is a small player compared to those, and it looks like Samsung wants SK Hynix’s slice of the market for itself. Samsung can easily push its way into the segment if it decides to, with all of the money, the R&D, the foundry, and everything else.

Now, even though Samsung is a huge, impressive player, with incredible pricing power right now, there is one thing that won’t let Samsung trade like Micron, probably ever. This is the fact that Micron is a U.S.-based company. U.S. investors prefer U.S. companies. They prefer U.S.-listed shares. They prefer not to deal with ADRs, FX, or foreign exchange rules. Even for me personally, when I rated Samsung as a buy earlier this year, I did not buy it simply because it was annoying for me. I even looked into buying the Korean-listed shares directly, but the process was too complicated, so I did not buy. I know I’m not the only investor who thinks this way, so Samsung will always stay cheaper than Micron in terms of valuation multiples, no matter how strong Samsung actually is.

An interesting thing here is that just because Samsung is doing well doesn’t mean Micron is doing badly. Micron is also up about thirty percent this month and around three percent today, and this rally is absolutely supported by fundamentals. The shortage in memory is in its craziest stage right now, and both Samsung and Micron are receiving massive, long-term orders from data center clients who are trying to secure memory supply for multiple years ahead. I don’t see the demand slowing down at all, especially as we increase our dependence on semiconductors, electric vehicles, automation, robotics, and AI. All of these sectors require enormous amounts of memory, and this trend is not reversing.

A Quick Look At Samsung’s Latest Results

And for investors who want to understand Samsung’s overall picture beyond the price hike, the company’s latest quarter was very strong. Revenue reached 86.1 trillion won, operating profit climbed above 12 trillion won, memory shipments were extremely strong across HBM3E and high-density DDR5, and the company significantly increased its R&D spending. Samsung also raised its dividend again and maintained its policy for shareholder returns.

Our total revenue reached KRW 86.1 trillion, up by 15.4% quarter-on-quarter. In the DS division, sales increased by 19% sequentially with the memory business setting a new all-time high for quarterly sales driven by strong growth of HBM3E and server SSDs. In the DX division, revenue was up 11% quarter-on-quarter, thanks to launch effects of new folding phones and solid flagship sales. SG&A expenses came in at KRW 21.3 trillion. And SG&A expenses as a percentage of sales declined by 3.1 percentage points sequentially to 24.8%.

As of the end of the third quarter, year-to-date R&D expenses climbed to a record high of KRW 26.9 trillion, reflecting our commitment to innovation and long-term growth.

Operating profit totaled KRW 12.2 trillion, representing a sequential increase of KRW 7.5 trillion, and operating margin increased by 7.9 percentage points quarter-on-quarter to 14.1%, led by DS division with increased memory sales, improved foundry line utilization and significantly reduced onetime inventory value adjustments compared to the previous quarter. Meanwhile, in the DX division, an increase in sales of high value-added products such as Fold 7 contributed to the growth.

Conclusion

Even if you’re not particularly interested in owning Samsung because it’s a Korean company and it doesn’t trade in the U.S., today’s news is still incredibly important to follow if you’re a shareholder of any memory stock like Micron or if you’re just interested in the semiconductor space at all. This shortage will spill into many corners of the market, and for investors like us, it finally gives a real opportunity to watch how the memory cycle behaves in an AI-driven world. Until now, we’ve never actually seen how these cycles evolve when demand isn’t coming from traditional PCs or smartphones, but from massive AI clusters that grow in unpredictable waves. And if customers really do overbuy memory chips now that supply is tight, we might get our first real glimpse of what the “AI cycle” looks like, and personally, I find that extremely interesting.
